design_philosophy.core_loop: Control Yuko through 2D levels, battle enemies, jump between platforms, defeat boss encounters. Switch between three characters (Yuko, Cham, Valna) to leverage different weapon types and magical abilities for progression and strategic combat variety.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Navigating platforming challenges while managing character roster flexibility and boss pattern recognition. Character-locked boss fights create commitment decisions.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Teenage schoolgirl warrior wielding mystical Valis sword, commanding demon allies to prevent interdimensional invasion and protect both Earth and Vecanti. Progression toward godhood through dutiful service.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Forced character assignments on certain stages and during all boss encounters remove player choice at critical moments, creating designed friction. Character switching mechanic (new to series) positioned as core feature, not accessibility—different boss fights yield different cutscenes based on character selection, embedding narrative consequence into mechanical choice.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High variance via character selection strategy: Yuko offers highest attack strength, Valna specializes in powerful magic with weak physical attack, Cham balances with whip weapon. Stage-forced character assignments eliminate difficulty customization at specific bottlenecks.

progression.upgrade_system: Each of three playable characters features upgradable weapons (Yuko's chargeable energy wave, Cham's whip, Valna's magical blasts) and spell upgrades (lightning, fire, ice varieties). Upgrades persist across character switches.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Linear stage progression; new characters (Cham, Valna) unlocked through story progression and rescue sequences. Valis sword power escalates narratively, culminating in 'full potential' release late-game.
replayability_hooks: Three-character roster encourages replays with different combat strategies
Character-locked boss encounters produce alternative cutscenes
Genesis version contains different level roster than TurboGrafx-CD original, creating platform-variant content

world_building_techniques: Animated cutscenes (TurboGrafx-CD version) interrupt gameplay at story beats
Character-specific boss fight cutscenes embed narrative branching into mechanical choice
Rescue mission narrative structure (retrieving Cham from kidnapping, Valna from captivity) motivates stage progression
Climactic final narrative moment: protagonist ascends to godhood, permanently departing Earth—story closure with mechanical consequence

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters serve as narrative pivots and character-forcing decision points. Final fight features returning antagonist (Rogles) alongside primary threat (Glames), creating escalation callback. Character selection at boss phases produces narrative differentiation via cutscene variation.
notable_bosses: King Glames (primary antagonist, wields Leethus sword, leader of Dark World invasion)
Rogles (returning antagonist from first Valis game, final-phase boss)

faction_indicators: Dark World denizens seeking refuge amid planetary destruction (sympathetic antagonist motivation)
Vecanti ruler Nizetti and celestial governance structure
Earth as homeland threatened by interdimensional conquest
danger_signals: King Glames seeks to conquer 'both Vecanti and Earth' — existential dual-world threat
Dark World planet destruction as forcing function for invasion narrative
Cham's father Lada killed by Glames for resistance — authoritarian suppression of dissent
environmental_storytelling: Yuko's narrative arc from warrior guardian to goddess reflects world state progression
Multiple rescues (Cham kidnapped, Valna captive) establish power imbalances requiring collaborative restoration
Valis sword progression from tool to 'unleashed full potential' weapon mirrors protagonist empowerment narrative
